title: The AI race moved up to the software layer above the model
summary: Named June moves — SpaceX's compute IPO, OpenAI buying Ona, labs and startups racing into agent software — show the model commoditizing while the software around the agent becomes the layer worth owning.
takeaways: Build and own the software layer around the agent — its context, tools, and controls — and treat the model as a swappable input behind a routing seam.

The model is the part that commoditizes: open models win token volume at about 1% of spend and the adoption leader flips month to month, so a stack wired to one model is anchored to the fastest-moving piece.
Route every model call through one gateway you control and keep context and controls model-agnostic, so a swap for a price cut or an outage is a config change instead of a rewrite.

sources.title: Vercel: AI Gateway Production Index, June 2026
SiliconANGLE: OpenAI acquires agent-orchestration startup Ona
TechCrunch: SpaceX to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock
Cohere: North Mini Code (first agentic coding model)
IBM: CIOs/CTOs face growing AI control gap
Kore.ai: enterprise AI agent risk survey

body:
## What happened

This June the AI race split away from the model in three directions at once. Underneath, compute became the moat: SpaceX absorbed xAI and priced the [largest IPO in history](https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/11/spacex-officially-prices-shares-at-135-in-the-largest-ipo-ever/), then rented bridge GPU capacity to Google at [920 million dollars a month](https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/05/google-will-pay-spacex-920m-per-month-for-compute/). On top, value moved into the software: OpenAI [bought the agent-runtime startup Ona](https://siliconangle.com/2026/06/11/openai-acquires-ai-agent-orchestration-startup-ona/), SpaceX [bought the coding tool Cursor for sixty billion dollars](https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/16/spacex-to-acquire-cursor-for-60b-in-stock-days-after-blockbuster-ipo/), Cohere shipped [its first agentic coding model](https://cohere.com/blog/north-mini-code), and startups raised straight into the layer — agent identity at [NewCore](https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/15/ai-agents-are-becoming-employees-newcore-emerges-with-66m-to-give-them-identities/), agent context at [Jedify](https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/10/jedify-raises-24m-to-help-companies-arm-ai-agents-with-context-on-their-business/). In the middle, the model commoditized: Anthropic edged past OpenAI in US business adoption [41% to 39.5%](https://ramp.com/leading-indicators/ai-index-june-2026), OpenAI's leaked financials showed [revenue near 13 billion dollars with larger losses](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/06/leaked-financial-docs-show-openai-is-losing-billions-of-dollars-a-year/), and on Vercel's gateway DeepSeek jumped [from under 1% to 17% of tokens at about 1% of spend](https://vercel.com/blog/ai-gateway-production-index-june-2026).

## Why it matters

Wire your app straight to one provider's API and you anchor it to the fastest-moving part of the stack. The cheapest capable model changes every few weeks and the leader flips month to month, so the model behaves like a commodity input. What does not commoditize is the software around the agent: the context you ground it with, the tools it can call, and the checks on what it does. That is the layer the labs are buying and the startups are building, and it is the layer you can actually own.

## How it works

1. **Put a routing seam in front of the model.** Every call goes through one gateway you control, so the provider becomes a config value rather than a hard dependency.
2. **Keep context in your layer.** Retrieval, memory, and grounding live in your code rather than the model's, so they survive a swap.
3. **Keep tools model-agnostic.** Define each tool once behind the seam; do not let provider-specific calling formats leak into your app.
4. **Route on cheapest-capable.** Send each task to the lowest-priced model that passes, and fail over to a backup when one errors.
5. **Put controls at the seam.** Spend caps, the audit log, and least-privilege keys live at the one choke point every call passes through.

> Treat the model as the part you rent, and the software around it as the part you own.

## What broke

This is advice for the teams that build on models. The few with the capital to win the compute war play a different game: they integrate vertically — Cursor, the harness winner, built its own [Composer model](https://cursor.com/blog/composer) and the labs are buying harnesses — because no single layer is a moat alone. And the governance layer is where teams are most exposed today: IBM found [77% say adoption outpaces governance](https://newsroom.ibm.com/2026-06-08-new-ibm-study-finds-cios-and-ctos-face-growing-ai-control-gap-as-enterprise-deployment-scales), Bain [only 7% run fully autonomous agents in production](https://www.bain.com/insights/your-ai-budget-is-growing-your-returns-arent-heres-why/), and [79% had already reversed an agent's action](https://www.kore.ai/news/new-kore-ai-survey-72-of-enterprises-say-their-ai-agents-operate-with-unmanaged-risk-and-create-new-operational-burdens). The seam is more than a cost lever; it is where that control lives.

[Architecture & Orchestration](/guide/architecture-and-orchestration/)
